threats.title: Validator Whitelist Permanently Removable During Extended Pause via removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk
threats.hypothesis_description: The function removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() has no whenNotPaused modifier. If the rollup admin pauses the contract for a legitimate operational reason (e.g., security incident, upgrade coordination) lasting longer than VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS (201600 blocks, ~28 days at 12s), any address can call this function to permanently and irreversibly disable the validator whitelist. Once unpaused, all validator-gated functions become permissionless, allowing any entity to stake, create assertions, and participate in the rollup without authorization.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect: the pausable guard is missing on a critical state-changing function. During extended maintenance windows, an attacker could exploit this to remove the whitelist before the admin intends.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() is external with no whenNotPaused modifier; block.number keeps incrementing during pause, making _validatorIsAfk() return true

preconditions: validatorWhitelistDisabled == false
Contract is paused by admin
Pause duration exceeds VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS (201600 blocks)
No new assertions confirmed during the pause

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk lacks whenNotPaused while all other state-mutating validator functions include it
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin pause for extended maintenance could trigger unintended whitelist removal
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the contract is paused for >28 days and no assertions are confirmed

operational_questions: Can the admin re-enable the whitelist after it's been disabled?
Is there a mechanism to extend the AFK threshold during planned maintenance?
What is the longest historical pause duration for any Arbitrum rollup?
validation_requirements: Confirm no admin function exists to re-enable the whitelist once disabled
Test calling removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk while contract is paused
Verify block.number advancement during paused state

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for rollup to be paused for operational reasons (security incident, upgrade)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Monitor block.number until VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS pass since last assertion activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() - succeeds even during pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Once contract is unpaused, any address can now act as validator
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single transaction by any address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent irreversible removal of validator whitelist
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Non-whitelisted entities can create assertions and participate in challenges
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery mechanism exists to re-enable the whitelist
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All validator-gated functions: stakeOnNewAssertion, confirmAssertion, returnOldDeposit, reduceDeposit, addToDeposit, createLayerZeroEdge

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must wait for 201600 blocks of pause duration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor pause events and block count since last confirmed assertion

threats.title: Rollup Token Accounting Desynchronization: amountStaked Not Reduced on Escrow Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: When stakeOnNewAssertion creates a non-first-child assertion, requiredStake tokens are transferred from the rollup contract to loserStakeEscrow. However, the creating staker's amountStaked is NOT reduced. This creates a persistent accounting desynchronization where sum(amountStaked for all stakers) + totalWithdrawableFunds > contract token balance. While the losing staker is typically stuck and cannot withdraw, if they can become inactive via a third party extending their losing branch, the phantom balance becomes actionable and the contract may have insufficient tokens to service all legitimate withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly acknowledges 'if the losing staker have staked more than requiredStake, the excess stake will be stuck' but doesn't adjust accounting. This creates an invariant violation of INV-ECON-001 where contract liabilities exceed assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransfer(loserStakeEscrow, requiredStake) executes without reducing _stakerMap[msg.sender].amountStaked; withdrawStaker later credits full amountStaked to withdrawable

preconditions: A fork exists (two children of the same assertion)
The losing staker has amountStaked >= requiredStake
The losing staker becomes inactive (their assertion gets a child)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token transfer to escrow with no corresponding reduction in staker accounting
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin intervention required - occurs in normal dispute flow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - occurs on every assertion fork where the loser becomes inactive

operational_questions: Under what conditions can a losing staker become inactive and trigger the accounting mismatch?
Can a losing staker use a sybil account to create a child on their losing assertion to become inactive?
What is the cumulative impact on contract solvency across multiple disputes?
validation_requirements: Simulate full dispute lifecycle: fork, escrow transfer, losing staker inactive, withdrawal attempt
Verify contract balance vs sum of amountStaked + totalWithdrawableFunds after dispute
Test whether losing staker's returnOldDeposit reverts due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Malicious validator creates a second child assertion (non-first-child) - requiredStake sent to escrow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Using a sybil address, create a child on the losing assertion to make the original staker inactive
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Original losing staker calls returnOldDeposit - full amountStaked credited to withdrawable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sybil account's deposit subsidizes the original staker's withdrawal
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Two accounts controlled by attacker, each with requiredStake

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inflated totalWithdrawableFunds creates phantom liabilities; late withdrawers may face reverts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain mechanism to correct accounting discrepancy

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: returnOldDeposit calls from stakers on losing branches
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Sybil account creating children on losing branches

threats.title: EdgeChallengeManager Lacks Emergency Pause Mechanism Allowing Continued Exploit During Rollup Pause
threats.hypothesis_description: EdgeChallengeManager does not inherit Pausable and has no pause/emergency stop mechanism. While RollupUserLogic can be paused to prevent assertion confirmation, the challenge manager continues to accept edge creation, bisection, timer updates, and edge confirmation. During a security incident requiring a pause, an attacker can continue to manipulate the challenge state (creating edges, accumulating timer, confirming edges), such that when the rollup is unpaused, the attacker has a confirmed winning edge ready to force confirmation of a malicious assertion.
threats.investigation_rationale: The architectural gap between RollupUserLogic (pausable) and EdgeChallengeManager (not pausable) creates an asymmetric response window where attackers can advance challenges while defenders cannot confirm assertions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract inherits only Initializable, not PausableUpgradeable; no pause() or unpause() functions; no whenNotPaused modifiers on any function

preconditions: RollupUserLogic is paused due to security incident
Active challenges exist in EdgeChallengeManager
Attacker has created edges prior to pause

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All EdgeChallengeManager state-mutating functions remain callable during rollup pause
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin cannot halt challenge activity even during critical security incidents
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Entire duration of any rollup pause

operational_questions: Can the admin replace the challenge manager address to orphan active challenges?
Is there a proxy admin that could upgrade the EdgeChallengeManager to add pause?
What is the impact of a confirmed malicious edge during pause?
validation_requirements: Confirm no indirect pause mechanism exists via assertionChain interface
Test full challenge lifecycle during rollup pause
Verify that edge confirmation during pause enables assertion confirmation upon unpause

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create and advance challenge edges before or during rollup pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Continue bisection and timer accumulation while rollup is paused
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Confirm malicious edge via time or one-step proof during pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When rollup is unpaused, use confirmed edge to immediately confirm malicious assertion
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Active challenge with valid edge structure

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Admin cannot halt challenge manipulation during security incidents
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Attacker advances challenge state while honest validators are blocked from confirming assertions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: May require proxy upgrade of EdgeChallengeManager to add pause functionality

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Edge confirmations occurring while rollup is paused
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Challenge activity spikes during pause events
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Edge creation and confirmation concentrated during paused periods

threats.title: EdgeChallengeManager Configuration Immutability Prevents Emergency Parameter Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: After initialization, EdgeChallengeManager has NO setter functions for any configuration parameter: assertionChain, challengePeriodBlocks, oneStepProofEntry, stakeToken, excessStakeReceiver, stakeAmounts, layer zero heights, or NUM_BIGSTEP_LEVEL. If a bug is discovered in the oneStepProofEntry, or staking parameters need emergency adjustment, or the excessStakeReceiver address is compromised, the only remedy is deploying a new EdgeChallengeManager entirely. Existing challenges in the old manager would be orphaned, and assertions referencing the old challenge manager via configHash would remain permanently unresolvable through the new manager.
threats.investigation_rationale: The complete absence of admin setters in a complex protocol component is a significant operational risk. The configHash binding in assertions means old assertions cannot use a new challenge manager.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initialize() sets all configuration; no other setter functions exist in the contract; no admin role or owner pattern for parameter updates

preconditions: A bug is discovered in oneStepProofEntry or other configured dependency
OR excessStakeReceiver address needs changing
OR stakeAmounts need adjustment

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Zero admin functions for parameter updates post-initialization
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Full redeployment required for any parameter change
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - any discovered bug in dependencies cannot be hotfixed

operational_questions: Is there a proxy admin that can upgrade the implementation to add setters?
What happens to in-flight challenges when a new challenge manager is deployed?
Can assertions with old configHash ever be resolved through a new challenge manager?
validation_requirements: Verify no proxy upgrade path exists that preserves challenge state
Assess impact of orphaned challenges on rollup liveness
Test whether old assertions can ever be confirmed if challenge manager changes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Discover or exploit a bug in oneStepProofEntry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Invalid SmallStep edges can be confirmed via the buggy prover
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Admin cannot fix the oneStepProofEntry reference without full redeployment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit window persists until new challenge manager is deployed and all affected assertions expire

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires full redeployment plus potential rollup state migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All challenge and assertion confirmation operations

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Depends on the specific vulnerability being exploited
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced understanding of challenge protocol and oneStepProofEntry
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After discovery of a dependency bug

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Invalid edge confirmations via buggy oneStepProofEntry
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated confirmEdgeByOneStepProof calls with unusual proof data

threats.title: VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS Hardcoded for 12-Second Block Time Enables Premature Whitelist Removal on Faster Chains
threats.hypothesis_description: The constant VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS = 201600 is hardcoded assuming a 12-second Ethereum L1 block time (yielding ~28 days). When deployed on chains with faster block times (e.g., 2-second blocks on some L2s), this period becomes ~4.67 days instead of the intended 28 days. This dramatically reduces the time window before any address can call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() to permanently disable the validator whitelist, even during brief periods of validator inactivity that would normally be expected.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract is designed for deployment on multiple chains (Ethereum and Arbitrum per documentation). On Arbitrum's faster block production, the AFK threshold would be reached much sooner than intended.
deployment_assumptions: Contract deployed on a chain with block time significantly different from 12 seconds
threats.config_evidence: hardhat.config.ts shows deployment targets include Arbitrum chains (arb1, nova, arbGoerliRollup); RollupCore.sol uses _hostChainIsArbitrum flag

proof_of_signal.pattern: VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS = 201600 with comment 'assuming a 12 seconds block time'; no chain-aware block time adjustment

preconditions: Contract deployed on a chain with block time < 12 seconds
validatorWhitelistDisabled == false
Brief period of validator inactivity (shorter than intended 28 days)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hardcoded block count constant used across chains with different block times
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin ability to adjust VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS post-deployment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any period of validator inactivity exceeding the reduced effective duration

operational_questions: What is the actual block time on the target deployment chain?
What is the effective AFK duration in calendar time on each target chain?
Are there periods of expected inactivity (upgrades, holidays) that could approach the reduced threshold?
validation_requirements: Calculate effective AFK duration for each target chain
Compare against expected validator downtime scenarios
Verify whether Arbitrum L2 block times would make this exploitable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a deployment on a chain with fast block times
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for a period of validator inactivity (shorter than intended 28 days)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() when effective threshold is reached
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Participate as non-whitelisted validator in the now-open rollup

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent whitelist removal before intended security threshold
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Non-authorized entities can participate in validation

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After reduced AFK threshold on fast-block chains

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Whitelist removal occurring much sooner than 28 days of inactivity
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Monitoring of _validatorIsAfk() return value on fast-block chains

threats.title: Losing Staker Excess Stake Permanently Locked with No Recovery Path
threats.hypothesis_description: When a validator creates a non-first-child assertion via stakeOnNewAssertion, requiredStake is transferred to loserStakeEscrow, but the staker's amountStaked is not reduced. If the staker deposited more than requiredStake (via addToDeposit or initial deposit), the excess (amountStaked - requiredStake) becomes permanently locked in the contract with no recovery mechanism. The staker cannot call returnOldDeposit (assertion has no child) or reduceDeposit (stake is active). There is no admin function to recover these stuck funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Code comment explicitly acknowledges: 'if the losing staker have staked more than requiredStake, the excess stake will be stuck'. This is a known but unresolved code defect that locks user funds permanently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Comment on line 161-162: 'NOTE: if the losing staker have staked more than requiredStake, the excess stake will be stuck'; no amountStaked reduction after safeTransfer to loserStakeEscrow

preconditions: Validator has amountStaked > requiredStake
Validator creates a non-first-child assertion (fork scenario)
The validator's assertion is ultimately on the losing branch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token transfer to escrow without corresponding balance reduction; no admin recovery function
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function exists to recover stuck tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - every dispute fork with over-staked validators

operational_questions: What percentage of validators stake more than requiredStake?
What is the total value at risk of being permanently locked?
Could a reduceDeposit call be made before stakeOnNewAssertion to avoid excess?
validation_requirements: Quantify the stuck funds scenario with realistic staking amounts
Verify no path exists for the losing staker to ever recover excess
Test whether the staker can reduceDeposit before creating the fork assertion

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack per se, but occurs in normal dispute flow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Validator with excess stake creates non-first-child assertion
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: requiredStake sent to escrow, excess remains locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If validator's assertion loses, their excess tokens are permanently inaccessible
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - natural consequence of the staking design

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Validators lose excess funds beyond requiredStake on losing forks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism exists; tokens are permanently locked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Stakers with amountStaked significantly above requiredStake on losing branches
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing locked token balance in rollup contract
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Validators adding excess deposit before forks

threats.title: confirmEdgeByOneStepProof machineStep Overflow with High numBigStepLevel Configuration
threats.hypothesis_description: In confirmEdgeByOneStepProof, the machineStep calculation iterates through BigStep levels, multiplying stepSize by bigStepHeight at each level: stepSize *= bigStepHeight. With numBigStepLevel > ~9 and bigStepHeight >= 2^26 (a power of 2), the intermediate value overflows uint256 (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic), causing the function to always revert. This permanently prevents one-step proof confirmation, making the challenge protocol unable to resolve disputes. The contract allows numBigStepLevel up to 253, but overflow occurs at much lower values.
threats.investigation_rationale: The multiplication stepSize *= bigStepHeight grows exponentially with the number of levels. For common bigStepHeight values (powers of 2 like 2^23 or 2^26), overflow occurs at modest level counts. The contract's max check (_numBigStepLevel > 253) is far too permissive.
deployment_assumptions: numBigStepLevel is set high enough that stepSize * bigStepHeight^(numBigStepLevel-1) overflows uint256
threats.config_evidence: EdgeChallengeManager.sol initialize(): _numBigStepLevel validated only as > 0 and <= 253; common.ts validates numBigStepLevel > 0 but no overflow check; foundry.toml confirms solc 0.8.17 with checked arithmetic

proof_of_signal.pattern: stepSize *= bigStepHeight in loop iterating through BigStep levels; exponential growth with no overflow guard

preconditions: numBigStepLevel configured high enough to cause overflow (depends on bigStepHeight)
For bigStepHeight = 2^26: numBigStepLevel >= 10 causes overflow
For bigStepHeight = 2^23: numBigStepLevel >= 12 causes overflow
Challenge reaches SmallStep one-step proof stage

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Exponential multiplication without overflow protection in critical challenge resolution path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Parameter set at initialization; cannot be corrected without redeployment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent if deployed with overflow-causing parameters

operational_questions: What are the typical/planned numBigStepLevel and bigStepHeight values for production deployments?
Is there a mathematical bound check in the deployment tooling?
Would overflow permanently break dispute resolution?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact overflow threshold for each combination of bigStepHeight and numBigStepLevel
Verify that production configurations stay below the overflow threshold
Test confirmEdgeByOneStepProof with boundary configurations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify deployment with numBigStepLevel high enough to cause overflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create dispute that requires one-step proof resolution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: confirmEdgeByOneStepProof always reverts due to overflow, challenge cannot resolve
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No valid assertion can be confirmed through challenge, potentially stalling rollup
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Must be staker to create dispute

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete inability to resolve challenges via one-step proofs; rollup dispute resolution permanently broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment of EdgeChallengeManager with corrected parameters

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime after deployment with vulnerable parameters

threats.title: fastConfirmNewAssertion Repeated Calls Drain Rollup Contract Token Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: fastConfirmNewAssertion can create assertions without requiring a staker deposit (no receiveTokens call). When the created assertion is not the first child, requiredStake is transferred from the rollup contract to loserStakeEscrow. A compromised anyTrustFastConfirmer could call this function repeatedly on the same predecessor, creating multiple non-first-child assertions, each draining requiredStake from the contract balance. This would exhaust the contract's tokens, preventing legitimate staker withdrawals. The code comments explicitly acknowledge this trust assumption.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function creates assertions and transfers stake without corresponding deposits. The code comments note: 'We trust the anyTrustFastConfirmer to not call this function multiple times on the same prev'. This is a documented trust assumption with catastrophic failure mode.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createNewAssertion called without staker deposit; safeTransfer(loserStakeEscrow, requiredStake) on non-first-child with no deposit to cover it; comment acknowledges trust assumption

preconditions: msg.sender == anyTrustFastConfirmer
anyTrustFastConfirmer address is compromised or malicious
Contract has token balance > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assertion creation path that transfers tokens out without corresponding deposit in
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the rollup is not paused and anyTrustFastConfirmer is compromised

operational_questions: What security controls protect the anyTrustFastConfirmer key/contract?
Can the admin replace anyTrustFastConfirmer immediately upon detecting compromise?
What is the maximum drainable amount?
validation_requirements: Verify that repeated fastConfirmNewAssertion calls drain balance
Quantify maximum drain rate per block
Test admin's ability to pause and replace fast confirmer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call fastConfirmNewAssertion repeatedly with same prev, each creating non-first-child
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each call transfers requiredStake to loserStakeEscrow from contract balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract balance depleted, legitimate stakers unable to withdraw

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full rollup state manipulation via fast confirmation bypass
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token drain prevents all staker withdrawals; rollup insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin intervention to pause and replace fast confirmer; drained funds may be unrecoverable

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: fastConfirmNewAssertion called more than once per predecessor

threats.title: getPrevAssertionHash Traversal Failure if Intermediate firstRivals is UNRIVALED
threats.hypothesis_description: getPrevAssertionHash traverses edge levels using store.firstRivals[edge.originId] to find the Block-level origin. The same traversal pattern is used in confirmEdgeByOneStepProof for machineStep calculation. If at any intermediate level firstRivals returns the UNRIVALED magic hash instead of a valid edge ID, the subsequent get() call reverts with EdgeNotExists. While the protocol's creation logic should prevent this (non-block layer zero edges require hasLengthOneRival on their claim), an edge case may exist where intermediate level rivals are resolved or where the traversal encounters an unexpected state, permanently blocking one-step proof resolution for the affected challenge.
threats.investigation_rationale: The traversal relies on firstRivals always containing valid edge IDs for intermediate levels. The code uses getNoCheck in some places and get() in others. A state where firstRivals[originId] == UNRIVALED at an intermediate level would be fatal for challenge resolution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while loop: lowerLevelId = store.firstRivals[edge.originId]; edge = get(store, lowerLevelId); -- if firstRivals returns UNRIVALED hash, get() reverts

preconditions: Multi-level challenge structure exists (numBigStepLevel > 0)
An intermediate level's firstRivals entry equals UNRIVALED
This requires a state where a claiming edge's originId maps to UNRIVALED in firstRivals

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Traversal uses firstRivals as edge ID without checking for UNRIVALED sentinel value
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin ability to fix corrupted firstRivals state
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: If the state condition is achievable, it permanently blocks OSP for that challenge

operational_questions: Can the edge creation logic ever produce a state where intermediate firstRivals is UNRIVALED?
Is the hasLengthOneRival check on claim edge creation sufficient to prevent this?
Could a confirmed rival edge leave firstRivals in an inconsistent state?
validation_requirements: Formal verification that layer zero edge creation guarantees firstRivals != UNRIVALED for all intermediate levels
Test getPrevAssertionHash with edge structures at all possible levels
Verify confirmEdgeByOneStepProof traversal with various level configurations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify if a sequence of edge operations can leave intermediate firstRivals as UNRIVALED
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If possible, create such a state via strategic edge creation patterns
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Any confirmEdgeByOneStepProof call traversing through this level reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The affected challenge cannot be resolved via OSP, potentially blocking assertion confirmation
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires deep understanding of edge creation ordering

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Specific challenges unable to resolve via one-step proof
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain fix; would require challenge manager redeployment

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires specific edge creation sequence

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Challenges stalling at SmallStep level with valid length-1 edges
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Strategic edge creation patterns at specific levels

threats.title: Assertion Confirmation Bypasses challengeManager Liveness Check via Stale configHash
threats.hypothesis_description: In confirmAssertion, the challengeManager address is obtained from prevConfig.challengeManager, which is validated against the prev assertion's configHash set at assertion creation time. If the EdgeChallengeManager is behind a proxy and the admin upgrades its implementation to a malicious or buggy version, the configHash validation still passes because it validates the ADDRESS (proxy), not the implementation. An upgraded challenge manager could return fabricated edge data, causing confirmAssertion to accept an invalid winning edge. The assertion chain immutably references the challenge manager proxy address, creating a persistent attack surface through implementation upgrades.
threats.investigation_rationale: The configHash binds to the challenge manager proxy address, not its implementation. Proxy upgrades change behavior without invalidating any configHash. This creates a subtle attack surface through the admin upgrade path.
deployment_assumptions: EdgeChallengeManager is deployed behind a proxy (implied by Initializable pattern)
Admin/upgrader can modify the proxy implementation
threats.config_evidence: EdgeChallengeManager.sol constructor calls _disableInitializers() indicating proxy deployment pattern; deployment.ts deploys challengeManager as template; upgrade/deploy4844.ts deploys 'ChallengeManager' without initialization suggesting proxy pattern

proof_of_signal.pattern: IEdgeChallengeManager(prevConfig.challengeManager).getEdge(winningEdgeId) - challengeManager address from configHash is the proxy address; proxy implementation can be upgraded without invalidating configHash

preconditions: EdgeChallengeManager deployed behind upgradeable proxy
Admin/upgrader compromised or malicious
Active assertions referencing the challenge manager proxy in their configHash

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: configHash validates proxy address not implementation; proxy upgrade changes getEdge() behavior
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin/upgrader to modify proxy implementation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After any proxy upgrade of EdgeChallengeManager

operational_questions: Is EdgeChallengeManager deployed behind a proxy in production?
Who controls the proxy upgrade rights?
Is there a timelock on proxy upgrades?
validation_requirements: Verify deployment pattern for EdgeChallengeManager
Check proxy admin controls and timelock configuration
Test assertion confirmation after challenge manager proxy upgrade

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Upgrade implementation to return fabricated edge data (confirmed status, matching claimId)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call confirmAssertion with a malicious assertionHash and the fabricated winningEdgeId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Invalid assertion confirmed, outbox updated with malicious state root

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Malicious assertion confirmation via fabricated challenge results
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Invalid L2 state finalized on L1; bridge integrity compromised
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Would require emergency pause and potential rollback
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: confirmAssertion and all dependent bridge operations

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low once proxy admin is compromised
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - must craft valid-looking edge data

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Proxy implementation upgrade events on EdgeChallengeManager
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Assertion confirmations referencing unexpected edge data
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proxy upgrades followed by rapid assertion confirmations
